items.prompt_md: In `dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a - c] + 1)`, what does adding 1 represent?
options: The one extra coin of denomination c being used to go from amount a-c up to amount a
A penalty applied whenever the amount cannot be made exactly
The number of coin denominations available
A fixed cost charged once per call to the function
items.answer: The one extra coin of denomination c being used to go from amount a-c up to amount a
items.explanation_md: Reaching amount a via coin c means taking the best way to reach a-c and using exactly one more coin (denomination c) on top of it.

items.prompt_md: Why is coin change called an 'unbounded' knapsack rather than a 0/1 knapsack?
options: Because each coin denomination can be reused any number of times when building the amount, unlike 0/1 items which are each used at most once
Because the amount can be any size, including unbounded amounts
Because there is no upper limit on the number of coin denominations
Because the dp array has no fixed length
items.answer: Because each coin denomination can be reused any number of times when building the amount, unlike 0/1 items which are each used at most once
items.explanation_md: 'Unbounded' refers to unlimited reuse of each choice, which is exactly what lets dp[a - c] be revisited by many different coins on the way to dp[a].

items.prompt_md: What is the correct base case for the coin change dp array, and why?
options: dp[0] = 0, because making an amount of zero requires zero coins
dp[0] = 1, because at least one coin must always be used
dp[0] = -1, signaling that zero is never reachable
dp[0] = len(coins), one attempt per denomination
items.answer: dp[0] = 0, because making an amount of zero requires zero coins
items.explanation_md: Every other dp[a] is built from dp[a - c] entries, so dp[0] = 0 is the anchor that makes the very first coin used count as exactly 1.

items.prompt_md: Why can dp[a] depend on dp[a - c] for any coin c, rather than a fixed lookback like dp[a-1] or dp[a-2]?
options: Because the set of coins that fit into amount a can have any denominations, so the useful previous indices vary with the coin set rather than being a fixed distance back
Because dp arrays in Python cannot use fixed-offset indexing
Because the amount is always a multiple of the smallest coin
Because unbounded problems require iterating backward through the array
items.answer: Because the set of coins that fit into amount a can have any denominations, so the useful previous indices vary with the coin set rather than being a fixed distance back
items.explanation_md: Unlike Fibonacci-shaped recurrences with a constant lookback window, coin change's dependencies are determined by whatever denominations exist, so the whole array (not a couple of rolling variables) is needed.

items.prompt_md: Why does a greedy strategy of always taking the largest coin that fits fail to find the minimum number of coins for arbitrary denominations?
options: Some coin systems (for example 1, 3, 4 making amount 6) have a combination using smaller coins that beats always grabbing the biggest one first
Greedy always produces the exact same answer as DP, just slower
Greedy fails only when the amount is odd
Greedy fails only if there is exactly one coin denomination
items.answer: Some coin systems (for example 1, 3, 4 making amount 6) have a combination using smaller coins that beats always grabbing the biggest one first
items.explanation_md: Greedy is only optimal for specially structured (canonical) coin systems like standard currency; for arbitrary denominations it can lock in a locally large coin that costs more coins overall.

items.prompt_md: A student writes the inner loop as `for c in coins: if c <= a: ...` but forgets the `if c <= a` guard. What goes wrong?
options: dp[a - c] would be indexed with a negative number, referencing the wrong end of the array instead of being skipped as infeasible
The loop would simply run one extra iteration with no effect on the answer
The program would raise a division by zero error
dp[a] would always end up equal to dp[0]
items.answer: dp[a - c] would be indexed with a negative number, referencing the wrong end of the array instead of being skipped as infeasible
items.explanation_md: When c > a, a - c is negative; without the guard, Python's negative indexing silently reads from the tail of the array instead of correctly treating that coin as unusable for amount a.

items.prompt_md: How should dp be initialized so that 'amount cannot be made' can be detected at the end?
options: Fill every entry except dp[0] with a sentinel larger than any achievable answer (like amount + 1), then check if dp[amount] still equals that sentinel
Fill every entry with 0, since 0 always means unreachable
Fill every entry with -1 and stop as soon as any -1 is overwritten
Leave the array uninitialized since Python fills lists with None by default
items.answer: Fill every entry except dp[0] with a sentinel larger than any achievable answer (like amount + 1), then check if dp[amount] still equals that sentinel
items.explanation_md: Because min() only ever lowers dp[a], starting with a value no real answer can reach lets you tell 'never updated' apart from 'reachable using 0 coins' at the end.

items.prompt_md: This lesson's minimum-coins version asks 'fewest coins to make amount a.' A different version asks 'how many distinct combinations make amount a.' Why does the loop order (coins outer, amount inner) start to matter for the combinations version but not for the minimum-coins version?
options: For counting combinations, coins outer prevents the same set of coins from being counted in more than one order, whereas minimum-coins only cares about the best count and is order-independent
Loop order never matters for either version; both always produce identical results regardless of nesting
Coins outer is required for minimum-coins only, and amount outer is required for combinations only
Loop order only matters if the coins list is unsorted
items.answer: For counting combinations, coins outer prevents the same set of coins from being counted in more than one order, whereas minimum-coins only cares about the best count and is order-independent
items.explanation_md: Amount-outer/coins-inner allows a coin to be used at different 'positions' in the fill order, which double-counts combinations as permutations; coins-outer commits to one canonical order per coin, so each combination is counted exactly once.

items.prompt_md: Why does top-down memoization (make(a) = 1 + min(make(a - c) for c in coins)) give the same answer as bottom-up tabulation here?
options: Both compute the same recurrence over the same subproblems — memoization just caches each make(a) the first time it's needed, while tabulation fills them in increasing order up front
Top-down and bottom-up solve different recurrences that happen to agree only on small amounts
Top-down is only correct if the coins list is sorted first
Bottom-up is an approximation and top-down is the exact method
items.answer: Both compute the same recurrence over the same subproblems — memoization just caches each make(a) the first time it's needed, while tabulation fills them in increasing order up front
items.explanation_md: The recurrence is identical either direction; the only difference is whether subproblems are solved on demand (top-down, cached) or in a predetermined order (bottom-up, tabulated).

items.prompt_md: Why is the number of coin denominations part of the time complexity, not just the amount?
options: Every amount from 1 to the target must be checked against every denomination, so both quantities multiply into the total work
The number of denominations only affects space, never time
Only the largest denomination affects the runtime, not the count of denominations
The number of denominations affects correctness but not performance
items.answer: Every amount from 1 to the target must be checked against every denomination, so both quantities multiply into the total work
items.explanation_md: The outer loop runs over every amount and the inner loop tries every coin at each amount, so the work is proportional to the product of the two, not just one of them.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the bottom-up tabulated coin change solution shown in this lesson, in terms of the amount n and the number of denominations k?
options: O(n * k)
O(n + k)
items.answer: O(n * k)
items.explanation_md: For each of the n amounts, the inner loop tries all k coins, so the total work is proportional to their product.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the bottom-up tabulated coin change solution?
options: O(n), for the dp array indexed by amount
O(1), since only one variable is tracked
O(k), one slot per coin
O(n * k), a full 2-D table
items.answer: O(n), for the dp array indexed by amount
items.explanation_md: Unlike the fixed two-entry lookback in Fibonacci-shaped DP, dp[a] can depend on any dp[a - c], so the full 1-D array of size amount + 1 must be kept.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the naive approach that recursively branches over every coin at every remaining amount without memoization?
options: O(k^n) in the worst case, exponential in the amount
O(n * k), same as the tabulated version
O(n log k)
O(k), independent of the amount
items.answer: O(k^n) in the worst case, exponential in the amount
items.explanation_md: Without caching, the same remaining amounts are recomputed across many different branches, causing the same kind of blowup as naive Fibonacci recursion, just with a branching factor of k instead of 2.

items.prompt_md: If the 'count the number of combinations' variant is solved with coins outer, amount inner, both loops over the same ranges as the minimum-coins version, what is its time complexity?
options: O(n * k), the same asymptotic complexity as the minimum-coins version
O(n + k), because combinations avoid revisiting coins
O(2^n), because counting requires exploring every combination explicitly
O(k^2), independent of the amount
items.answer: O(n * k), the same asymptotic complexity as the minimum-coins version
items.explanation_md: Swapping the loop order changes what gets counted (combinations vs. permutations), not how many cells get filled, so the asymptotic cost stays n times k.

items.prompt_md: You're given a set of coin denominations and a target amount, and must find the fewest coins (each denomination reusable any number of times) that sum to exactly that amount. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the amount, where dp[a] pulls from dp[a - c] for every coin c
Greedy: always take the largest coin that fits, repeating until the amount is reached
Two pointers scanning the sorted coin list from both ends
Binary search over the number of coins used
items.answer: 1-D DP over the amount, where dp[a] pulls from dp[a - c] for every coin c
items.explanation_md: Minimizing a count over reusable choices with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems (many coin paths reach the same intermediate amount) is the signature of 1-D DP; greedy fails for arbitrary denominations.

items.prompt_md: You're told the coin denominations are U.S. currency (1, 5, 10, 25) and must find the fewest coins for a target amount. Which approach fits?
options: Greedy: always take the largest coin that fits, since this specific denomination set is canonical and greedy is provably optimal for it
1-D DP over the amount, which is required no matter what the denominations are
Backtracking over every possible coin sequence
Binary search over the amount
items.answer: Greedy: always take the largest coin that fits, since this specific denomination set is canonical and greedy is provably optimal for it
items.explanation_md: This is a trap about scope, not a real exception: greedy happens to be optimal for specific well-behaved (canonical) coin systems like U.S. currency, but the general coin-change problem makes no such promise, so the safe, general-purpose tool that always works is still 1-D DP.

items.prompt_md: You must count the number of distinct combinations of reusable coin denominations that sum to a target amount (order doesn't matter). Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the amount with coins as the outer loop, accumulating counts instead of taking a minimum
Greedy: always add the smallest coin repeatedly until the amount is reached
Two pointers over a sorted list of coins
Binary search over the target amount
items.answer: 1-D DP over the amount with coins as the outer loop, accumulating counts instead of taking a minimum
items.explanation_md: Counting reachable ways with reusable choices is still an overlapping-subproblems, optimal-substructure setup — only the loop order and the aggregation (sum vs. min) change, not the underlying pattern.

items.prompt_md: You have an unlimited supply of tile lengths and must find the minimum number of tiles to exactly cover a hallway of a given total length. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the total length, where dp[len] pulls from dp[len - tile] for every tile length
Greedy: always place the longest tile that still fits, repeating until the hallway is covered
Backtracking over every ordering of tiles
Two pointers moving inward from both ends of the hallway
items.answer: 1-D DP over the total length, where dp[len] pulls from dp[len - tile] for every tile length
items.explanation_md: This is the same unbounded-reuse, minimize-over-a-sum shape as coin change wearing a different name — a reusable-tile version of the exact same trigger.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether a target amount can be made at all using a set of reusable coin denominations, without needing to know the minimum count. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the amount, where dp[a] is true if any dp[a - c] is true for some coin c
Binary search over the target amount to find where it becomes reachable
Two pointers scanning the sorted denominations
Greedy: always take the smallest coin until the remaining amount is zero or negative
items.answer: 1-D DP over the amount, where dp[a] is true if any dp[a - c] is true for some coin c
items.explanation_md: Dropping the 'minimum' requirement still leaves the same reusable-choice, reachability-over-a-sum structure, just with a boolean dp array instead of a numeric one.

items.prompt_md: You have a fixed set of items, each usable at most once, and must find whether some subset sums to exactly a target value. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the target sum, but iterating amounts in decreasing order per item so each item is only used once
1-D DP over the amount exactly as in coin change, iterating amounts upward with unlimited reuse of each item
Greedy: always add the largest remaining item that doesn't exceed the target
Two pointers scanning a sorted version of the items
items.answer: 1-D DP over the target sum, but iterating amounts in decreasing order per item so each item is only used once
items.explanation_md: This is a trap on the unbounded assumption: 0/1 subset-sum looks identical to coin change but each item can only be used once, so amounts must be swept downward per item to prevent reusing it — plain upward iteration silently turns it back into an unbounded knapsack.

items.prompt_md: You must generate every distinct combination of reusable coin denominations that sums to a target amount, not just count or minimize them. Which approach fits?
options: Backtracking: recursively try including each denomination (with reuse allowed) and undo the choice once a branch is exhausted, collecting every valid combination
1-D DP over the amount, since it's still a coin-based reusable-choice problem
Binary search over the number of combinations
Two pointers over the sorted denominations
items.answer: Backtracking: recursively try including each denomination (with reuse allowed) and undo the choice once a branch is exhausted, collecting every valid combination
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: DP answers a single count or optimum, but enumerating every actual combination means exploring and recording each distinct path, which needs backtracking rather than a dp table that collapses paths together.

items.prompt_md: You must find the minimum number of perfect-square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16, ...) that sum to a given target integer. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the target, where dp[n] pulls from dp[n - s] for every perfect square s no larger than n
Greedy: always subtract the largest perfect square that fits and repeat
Backtracking over every ordering of perfect squares
Binary search over the count of squares used
items.answer: 1-D DP over the target, where dp[n] pulls from dp[n - s] for every perfect square s no larger than n
items.explanation_md: Perfect squares are just another reusable 'denomination' set, so minimizing a count to reach a target is the same unbounded-knapsack shape as coin change.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of unique coin denominations, you must quickly check whether a specific denomination value exists in the array. Which approach fits?
options: Binary search over the sorted array for the target value
1-D DP over the amount, treating the lookup as a reachability problem
Greedy: scan from the largest denomination downward until a match or a smaller value is found
Two pointers converging from both ends of the array
items.answer: Binary search over the sorted array for the target value
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: this isn't a coin-change-shaped problem at all — it's a plain membership lookup in a sorted array, which binary search solves directly without any DP table.

items.prompt_md: You must find the fewest number of jumps to reach the end of an array, where from index i you may jump forward by any amount from 1 up to nums[i] steps. Which approach fits?
options: 1-D DP over the index, where dp[i] pulls from dp[i - s] for every valid jump size s reaching i (or a greedy range-extension optimization of the same idea)
Backtracking over every possible sequence of jumps
Two pointers moving from both ends of the array toward the middle
Binary search over the number of jumps
items.answer: 1-D DP over the index, where dp[i] pulls from dp[i - s] for every valid jump size s reaching i (or a greedy range-extension optimization of the same idea)
items.explanation_md: Minimizing a count (jumps) to reach a target index by combining reusable-style forward choices is the same overlapping-subproblem shape as coin change, just indexed by position instead of amount.
